message.content:
POLARIS SPEED-VS-QUALITY INVESTIGATION. Established facts (measured today):
- A deep report render (route_all = 328 of 329 baskets) takes ~30+ min END-TO-END, and the AGENTIC
  RETRIEVAL phase (the react loop fetching URLs one turn at a time) dominates (~24 min); the COMPOSE
  phase is the smaller slice.
- We ported compose_fix's 16-way concurrency into outline_agent. The OFF-LOOP to_thread wrap is
  verdict-safe. But the aggressive PG_COMPOSE_BASKET_WORKERS + semaphore(48) DEADLOCKED at full
  328-basket scale: 19/20 threads stuck in futex_wait, 0 progress 8.8min, had to SIGKILL. The small
  verdict-identity A/B passed but never exercised full-scale concurrency.
- Also observed: the outline can DEGRADE to seed+partial under load (must stay cp4_used=agentic).

QUALITY INVARIANTS ANY SPEEDUP MUST KEEP (non-negotiable):
1. DEEP COVERAGE: render ~328/329 baskets (route_all). Do NOT speed up by dropping baskets/coverage.
2. FAITHFULNESS: strict_verify gate intact; kept/dropped NLI verdict set unchanged; no unverified/
   derived number renders via [CITE:ev_xxx]. Speed must be verdict-identical.
3. AGENTIC: cp4_used=agentic, never degrade-to-seed as a shortcut.
4. NO DEADLOCK: a speedup that can hang is not a speedup.

READ-ONLY: read code in /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent and /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix; run cheap
deterministic profiling; write findings ONLY to /home/polaris/polaris_project/speed_investigation.md.
Do NOT edit either worktree (a wheel is live in outline_agent). Source key for any measured run:
set -a; . /workspace/POLARIS/.env; set +a. Report REAL measured numbers; if you can't measure, say so.


ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this proposed speedup. Does it REALLY save meaningful wall-time AND keep ALL 4 quality invariants (deep coverage, faithfulness verdict-identity, cp4_used=agentic, no-deadlock)? Default to keeps_quality=FALSE if it risks any invariant or the saving is marginal.

SPEEDUP: No change here; do NOT invest in intra-search fetch concurrency. Focus effort on R1/R2 (turn-level parallelism + exhaustion breaker), which target the real ~53% non-fetch overhead and ~24% zero-yield waste.
CLAIMED SAVING: 0 (avoids wasted effort)
STATED RISK: N/A — this is a negative finding that redirects effort away from a dead end; raising max_workers further would add provider load with no measured benefit.
EVIDENCE: live_retriever.py:6363-6441: parallel_fetch(max_workers=band 8-14 scaled by candidate count, per_task_timeout=120s, per-host cap 4). Measured per-search fetch 12.7-56.3s is bounded by the slowest URL in a ~8-23 URL batch, not sequential per-URL fetching.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

6340	                self._lock = threading.Lock()
6341	                self.results = fetched_side
6342	
6343	            def fetch(
6344	                self, task: "FetchTask"
6345	            ) -> tuple[bytes, str, int]:
6346	                # I-meta-007c: surface the per-candidate DOI/PMID hints
6347	                # (stashed in task_metadata at task construction) so the OA
6348	                # resolver can fire on the DEFAULT parallel path, not just the
6349	                # serial fallback.
6350	                _meta = task.task_metadata or {}
6351	                content, ok, title, body_type, jsonld = _fetch_content(
6352	                    task.source_url, self.max_chars,
6353	                    doi_hint=str(_meta.get("doi") or ""),
6354	                    pmid_hint=str(_meta.get("pmid") or ""),
6355	                )
6356	                with self._lock:
6357	                    self.results[task.source_url] = (
6358	                        content, ok, title, body_type, jsonld,
6359	                    )
6360	                payload = (content or "").encode("utf-8", errors="replace")
6361	                return (payload, "text/plain", 200 if ok else 502)
6362	
6363	        # I-fetch-003 (#1175 / BB5-C02): scale max_workers with the candidate
6364	        # count instead of a flat default-8. Explicit env wins; when UNSET,
6365	        # min(_CEILING, max(_FLOOR, len(candidates) // _PER_CANDIDATE)). Named
6366	        # constants (LAW VI — no magic numbers). With the I-fetch-lock ceiling
6367	        # of 14 a large corpus lands at 14 workers (band 14-16); small corpora
6368	        # stay at the floor of 8.
6369	        _explicit_workers = os.environ.get("PG_LIVE_RETRIEVER_MAX_WORKERS")
6370	        if _explicit_workers is not None:
6371	            try:
6372	                max_workers = max(1, int(_explicit_workers))
6373	            except ValueError:
6374	                max_workers = _FETCH_WORKERS_FLOOR
6375	        else:
6376	            max_workers = min(
6377	                _FETCH_WORKERS_CEILING,
6378	                max(
6379	                    _FETCH_WORKERS_FLOOR,
6380	                    len(candidates) // _FETCH_WORKERS_PER_CANDIDATE,
6381	                ),
6382	            )
6383	        try:
6384	            per_task_timeout = float(os.environ.get(
6385	                "PG_LIVE_RETRIEVER_FETCH_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "120",
6386	            ))
6387	        except ValueError:
6388	            per_task_timeout = 120.0
6389	
6390	        # I-fetch-003 (#1175 / BB5-C02): per-HOST politeness limit. The
6391	        # parallel_fetch semaphore is keyed by FetchTask.backend_id; keying it
6392	        # by the URL host (below) gives each host its own Semaphore so distinct
6393	        # hosts fetch concurrently while same-host stays capped. Env-overridable;
6394	        # default mirrors parallel_fetch DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT (4).
6395	        _per_host_limit = _env_int(
6396	            "PG_LIVE_RETRIEVER_PER_HOST_CONCURRENT",
6397	            _PARALLEL_FETCH_DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT,
6398	        )
6399	
6400	        fetch_tasks = []
6401	        _per_host_concurrent: dict[str, int] = {}
6402	        for idx, c in enumerate(candidates):
6403	            # I-meta-007c: carry the candidate's DOI/PMID hints into the
6404	            # FetchTask so _LiveContentParallelFetcher.fetch can pass them to
6405	            # _fetch_content for the OA resolver (default = parallel path).
6406	            _doi, _pmid = _candidate_oa_hints(getattr(c, "metadata", None))
6407	            # I-fetch-003 (#1175): key the rate-limit class by URL host so the
6408	            # parallel_fetch host-semaphore yields cross-host parallelism.
6409	            _host = urlsplit(c.url).hostname or "default"
6410	            _per_host_concurrent[_host] = _per_host_limit
6411	            fetch_tasks.append(
6412	                FetchTask(
6413	                    source_url=c.url,
6414	                    backend_id=_host,
6415	                    task_metadata={"index": idx, "doi": _doi, "pmid": _pmid},
6416	                )
6417	            )
6418	        fetcher = _LiveContentParallelFetcher(DEFAULT_CONTENT_MAX_CHARS)
6419	        # I-deepfix-001 (wall/tiering-abort fix, #1344): bound the fetch batch to a
6420	        # FRACTION of the remaining retrieval wall so slow web-fetch cannot consume the
6421	        # ENTIRE wall and leave the post-fetch classify loop (which builds the corpus and
6422	        # defers W5 credibility-tiering) zero time — the starvation that produced an
6423	        # all-rules-floor T4-skewed corpus and a FALSE abort_corpus_approval_denied. The
6424	        # remainder of the wall is reserved for classification. Still hard-capped by the
6425	        # wall itself (fraction <= 1.0); PG_RETRIEVAL_FETCH_WALL_FRACTION=1.0 reproduces
6426	        # the legacy full-wall budget byte-for-byte. Faithfulness-neutral (§-1.3): a source
6427	        # not fetched before the cap is DISCLOSED via retrieval_wall_hit/notes, never
6428	        # silently dropped.
6429	        _fetch_wall_fraction = _retrieval_fetch_wall_fraction()
6430	        _fetch_now_mono = time.monotonic()
6431	        _fetch_deadline = min(
6432	            _retrieval_deadline,
6433	            _fetch_now_mono
6434	            + _fetch_wall_fraction
6435	            * max(0.0, _retrieval_deadline - _fetch_now_mono),
6436	        )
6437	        parallel_report = parallel_fetch(
6438	            fetch_tasks, fetcher,
6439	            max_workers=max_workers,
6440	            per_backend_max_concurrent=_per_host_concurrent,
6441	            per_task_timeout=per_task_timeout,
6442	            # I-deepfix-001 P1-3 (#1344): bound the fetch batch budget by the REMAINING
6443	            # retrieval-phase wall. `_retrieval_deadline` is an absolute monotonic instant
6444	            # (same domain as parallel_fetch's batch_start), so the effective batch
6445	            # deadline = min(its derived budget, the wall). Without this the derived
6446	            # budget (per_task_timeout * waves ≈ 3960s at FETCH_CAP=740) dwarfs the
6447	            # 1800s wall and the wall could not cap the fetch grind.
6448	            # I-deepfix-001 (wall/tiering-abort fix): use the fraction-reserved
6449	            # `_fetch_deadline` (<= `_retrieval_deadline`) so a classify slice survives.
6450	            overall_deadline_monotonic=_fetch_deadline,
6451	        )
6452	        # Run-log evidence: persist the substrate's report into
6453	        # api_calls so the manifest sees a non-zero invocation count.
6454	        api_calls["parallel_fetch_success_count"] = (
6455	            parallel_report.success_count
6456	        )
6457	        api_calls["parallel_fetch_errored_count"] = (
6458	            parallel_report.errored_count
6459	        )

content.content:
21:  - parallel_fetch substrate EXISTS but NOT wired to live_retriever (phase-1 only):
22:      audit_ir/parallel_fetch.py header. retrieval_cache has eviction API: audit_ir/retrieval_cache.py.
77:     max_workers=N) at verified_compose.py:2981/2992; each map thread calls verify_fn -> NLI POST
105:     retry-exhaustion fail-CLOSED drops -> MORE claims verified with SAME verdicts,
254:    parallel_fetch(max_workers=band 8-14, per_task_timeout=120s, per-host cap 4). So the
263:      ~7-62s). The ~391s of non-fetch LLM overhead is largely the between-fetch checklist +
281:  R1 [BIG, safe-with-guards]  PARALLEL GAP DRAIN + single checklist per round.
298:  R2 [MEDIUM, safe]  PER-ASPECT URL-EXHAUSTION BREAKER + intra-run query/URL-set cache.
302:     and re-firing. Directly kills the W2 179s zero-yield stretch. Est saving ~2-3 min.
314:     lower priority than R1/R2.
318:  W2 breaker (R2) achieves the same wall savings WITHOUT dropping depth, so prefer it.

240	      step2=898s, step3_treatment=922s, _16way_run=1062s, step4=737s(loop only).
241	      => retrieval/outline phase is consistently ~12-18 min. (Brief's "~24 min" is the
242	      high end / a heavier run; I could not reproduce 24 min in the logs on hand, but
243	      retrieval is unambiguously the dominant phase every time.)
244	
245	REACT-LOOP INTERNALS (measured):
246	  - 13 search_more_evidence calls; total FETCH wall = 346.3s; total NEW rows kept = 42.
247	  - Non-fetch LLM overhead inside the loop = 737 - 346 = ~391s (53% of the loop):
248	      _decide (reasoning_effort=high, max_tokens=131072, reasoning_max=32768; decide wall 660s)
249	      + after_fold full-section checklist (ran 11x, each an LLM per section)
250	      + fold-in per-row S2 topic-judge.
251	    Per turn that is ~20-24s of LLM latency that is NOT fetching; one post-bounce _decide
252	    alone took ~62s (04:22:34 -> ~04:23:36).
253	  - WITHIN a single search, URL fetch is ALREADY parallel: live_retriever.py:6363-6441
254	    parallel_fetch(max_workers=band 8-14, per_task_timeout=120s, per-host cap 4). So the
255	    per-search 12-56s is bounded by the slowest URL in the batch, NOT sequential fetching.
256	    => lack of intra-search fetch concurrency is NOT the bottleneck.
257	
258	THREE CONCRETE WASTE SOURCES (measured, in priority order):
259	
260	  W1. TURN-LEVEL SERIALIZATION. The seed checklist named 3 gaps AT ONCE (04:13:05) but the
261	      loop drains them ONE gap per turn, re-running the full per-section after_fold checklist
262	      LLM between every fetch. 13 sequential turns * (fetch ~27s + checklist ~10-15s + decide
263	      ~7-62s). The ~391s of non-fetch LLM overhead is largely the between-fetch checklist +
264	      decide serialization.
265	
266	  W2. ZERO-YIELD REDUNDANT RE-FETCHES. Four consecutive searches 04:20:01/04:20:36/04:21:08/
267	      04:21:59 for the SAME aspect ("impacts across various industries", re-worded each time)
268	      each fetched 8-10 URLs but kept=0 — every URL was a url-dup already in ev_store
269	      (url-dup dropped 9/9, 10/10, 8/8, 9/9). The whole 04:19:35 -> 04:22:34 stretch = 179s
270	      (24% of the react loop) produced ZERO new rows. Serper returns the same top URLs for
271	      near-identical queries; there is no query->URL-set cache and no per-aspect "URL set
272	      already exhausted -> stop re-searching" breaker at the fetch layer (the section-veto at
273	      outline_agent.py:1630 only covers structurally-unhomeable SECTIONS, not exhausted aspects).
274	
275	  W3. HEAVY DECIDE. _decide uses reasoning_effort=high + max_tokens=131072 for what is a menu
276	      pick over ~5 tools; it is the variable tail (one call ~62s). query_derive also carries
277	      max_tokens=131072 default (outline_agent.py:134) though bounded by reasoning_max.
278	
279	PROPOSED SPEEDUPS (retrieval lane; coverage + faithfulness safe):
280	
281	  R1 [BIG, safe-with-guards]  PARALLEL GAP DRAIN + single checklist per round.
282	     Fetch the N PENDING ledger gaps concurrently (bounded 3-4), then fold-in SEQUENTIALLY in a
283	     deterministic order and run the after_fold checklist ONCE per batch instead of once per
284	     fetch. Collapses ~13 sequential turns into ~4-5 batched rounds and removes the redundant
285	     between-fetch checklists. Est saving ~3-5 min.
286	     QUALITY: coverage unchanged (same gaps fetched); search_more_evidence only ADDS candidate
287	     rows — the strict_verify + NLI kept/dropped set is computed downstream and is unchanged.
288	     RISK/GUARD: (a) fold_in_fetched_rows (url-dedup -> offset-renumber hard assert -> S2 stamp)
289	     is ORDER-SENSITIVE, so keep fold-in SERIAL in a canonical (sorted url/candidate-key) order —
290	     parallelize only the FETCHES. (b) Give the fan-out its OWN dedicated ThreadPoolExecutor,
291	     NOT asyncio.to_thread's shared loop-default pool — this is exactly the shared-executor
292	     nesting that produced the 328-scale futex deadlock; cap concurrency low (3-4) since the
293	     outline agent runs ONCE per report (not per basket) so it needs no aggressive fan-out.
294	     (c) contain fan-out exceptions per-round (the :740 try/except already returns a failed
295	     ToolResult) so nothing escapes to :2054 and trips degrade-to-seed. MUST A/B verdict-identity
296	     at full 328 scale + assert cp4_used=="agentic".
297	
298	  R2 [MEDIUM, safe]  PER-ASPECT URL-EXHAUSTION BREAKER + intra-run query/URL-set cache.
299	     Cache Serper query->URL-set within a run; when a derived query's candidate URL set is
300	     >=~90% already in ev_store, SKIP the fetch and route the aspect straight to
301	     UNFILLED+disclosed (the module already discloses at the retry cap) instead of re-wording
302	     and re-firing. Directly kills the W2 179s zero-yield stretch. Est saving ~2-3 min.
303	     QUALITY: does NOT drop any basket (baskets are the pre-existing 329 clusters; this only
304	     stops re-fetching evidence already pooled); disclosure preserved; verdicts unaffected.
305	     RISK/GUARD: key the breaker on URL-SET overlap AFTER the same url-dedup rule, never on query
306	     TEXT, so a genuinely-new query is never suppressed.
307	
308	  R3 [SMALL, needs A/B]  CHEAPER DECIDE. Drop the _decide call (only) to reasoning_effort=medium
309	     / smaller reasoning_max_tokens, and lower PG_OUTLINE_QUERY_DERIVE_MAX_TOKENS from 131072.
310	     Est saving ~30-90s.
311	     QUALITY: decide is a routing choice, not a faithfulness judgment; checklist critic + strict_
312	     verify + NLI untouched. RISK: it CAN change WHICH gaps are searched -> different evidence
313	     pool -> not strictly verdict-identical. Flag: gate behind full-scale verdict-identity A/B;
314	     lower priority than R1/R2.
315	
316	  DISQUALIFIED: a blanket lower PG_OUTLINE_AGENT_MAX_TURNS is a coverage cut (starves the gap
317	  ledger while pending+budget remain) — same verdict as the quality-audit lane's idea (3). The
318	  W2 breaker (R2) achieves the same wall savings WITHOUT dropping depth, so prefer it.
319

content.text:
I've verified the evidence behind this negative finding against both the live code and the measured investigation log.

**Code verification (live_retriever.py:6363-6441):** Confirmed. Intra-search URL fetch is already concurrent via `parallel_fetch`:
- `max_workers` scales with candidate count, band-limited by `_FETCH_WORKERS_FLOOR=8` / `_FETCH_WORKERS_CEILING=14` (constants at lines 149/156/157) → the claimed "band 8-14" is exact.
- Per-host politeness cap default 4 (`_PARALLEL_FETCH_DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT`), distinct hosts fetch in parallel (host-keyed semaphore).
- `per_task_timeout=120s`. So per-search wall is bounded by the slowest URL in the batch, not sequential per-URL fetching. The claim is accurate.

**Measured-numbers verification (speed_investigation.md:245-273):** The arithmetic corroborates the cited figures. Loop wall 737s, fetch wall 346s → non-fetch overhead 391s = **53%** (matches). Zero-yield stretch 179s / 737s = **24%** (matches). Per-search fetch range 12-56s matches the claimed 12.7-56.3s.

**Adversarial verdict:** This "speedup" is a negative finding — a recommendation to NOT build intra-search fetch concurrency and instead spend effort on R1 (turn-level parallel gap drain) and R2 (per-aspect exhaustion breaker). It is correct and well-supported:
- Intra-search concurrency already exists, so building it again yields ~0 wall-time. The real waste is the 53% non-fetch LLM overhead (turn serialization) and 24% zero-yield re-fetching — neither addressed by fetch-layer concurrency.
- Because it prescribes no code change, it cannot break any of the 4 invariants (coverage, faithfulness verdict-identity, cp4_used=agentic, no-deadlock). It also actively avoids a deadlock/provider-load risk: raising `max_workers` further would add same-scale futex contention (the very failure mode seen at 328-scale) for no measured benefit.

The honest caveat for the schema: the "saving" is not runtime wall-time (that's 0/marginal by the proposal's own admission) — it is avoided-wasted-engineering-effort. The finding keeps all quality invariants precisely because it is a no-op that redirects effort to R1/R2.

The only 'saving' is avoided wasted engineering effort plus avoided added provider load / futex-contention risk.

Per-search wall is bounded by the slowest URL in the batch, not sequential fetching. The measured breakdown corroborates the cited waste: loop 737s, fetch 346s -> 391s non-fetch = 53%; zero-yield stretch 179s/737s = 24%. So fetch-layer concurrency is provably NOT the bottleneck; the real waste is turn serialization + redundant re-fetch, which R1/R2 target. keeps_quality=TRUE because the finding prescribes no code change: coverage, faithfulness verdict-identity, cp4_used=agentic, and no-deadlock are all trivially preserved, and it further AVOIDS the deadlock/provider-load risk that raising max_workers would introduce. The finding is sound; the only honest correction is that its 'saving' is effort-avoidance, not measurable wall-time.
